stories.title: NYC BERS Expands to Full-Floor Lease at 55 Water Street
stories.excerpt: CBRE arranged a long-term, 78,079-square-foot office lease for the New York City Board of Education Retirement Systems (BERS) at 55 Water St. in Lower Manhattan. BERS previously occupied a portion of the building’s 50th floor as a subtenant and will now occupy the entire 50th floor and add a ground-floor retail presence under a new, 30-year direct lease with the property owner.  The CBRE team of Howard Fiddle, Bradley Gerla, Evan Haskell and William Hooks represented the property owner, New Water Street Corp., in the negotiations. Marc Shapses of Savills acted on behalf of BERS in the transaction. “BERS’ decision to commit to a 30-year lease is a strong endorsement of 55 Water Street and Lower Manhattan as a premier business destination,” said Fiddle.   Added Gerla, “With the successful conclusion of this transaction, the building has executed approximately 275,000 square feet of leases over the past year and a half.” The post NYC BERS Expands to Full-Floor Lease at 55 Water Street appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: BERS expands to full 50th floor + ground retail at 55 Water St., 30-year direct lease
Building has executed ~275k SF of leases in past 18 months, signaling strong Lower Manhattan office demand recovery
Long-term commitment to single large tenant stabilizes cash flow but reflects office market shift toward fewer, larger occupiers
stories.summary: NYC Board of Education Retirement Systems (BERS) signed a 30-year, 78,079-SF direct lease at 55 Water Street in Lower Manhattan, expanding from a subtenant position to occupy the entire 50th floor plus ground-floor retail. The property owner has leased approximately 275,000 SF in the past 18 months, indicating stabilization in Lower Manhattan's post-pandemic office market. The 30-year commitment is framed as validation of the submarket as a premier business destination.
stories.impact: Minimal impact on multifamily development. Office leasing activity does not directly affect apartment demand or supply; however, sustained office stability in Lower Manhattan could marginally support residential demand if it signals lasting employer retention and job growth in the submarket.

stories.title: Stream Realty Lists Single-Tenant Mission Viejo Office Property for Sale
stories.excerpt: Stream Realty Partners has been selected to market the sale of 20532 El Toro, a three-story, 61,418-square-foot office property in Mission Viejo, formerly known as Mission Foothills Professional Building. The property offers strong connectivity to Irvine, Lake Forrest, Foothill Ranch, and the greater Southern California region. The property has undergone extensive renovations, featuring creative office finishes with open plenum ceilings, modernized restrooms, and collaborative kitchen areas, complemented by a new roof, HVAC systems, plumbing, and electrical throughout.   “20532 El Toro presents a compelling opportunity for a wide range of buyers in South Orange County,” said Mike Adams, managing director at Stream. “Currently configured for a full-building occupier, the property offers flexibility for a multi-tenant environment through the potential demising of six pods, creating the opportunity to generate tenant income. Additional use scenarios include condo conversion, medical-adjacent conversion, and long-term strategic land banking. Prior ownership capital investment reduces near-term execution risk for a future buyer.” The post Stream Realty Lists Single-Tenant Mission Viejo Office Property for Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 61,418-SF office property in Mission Viejo, CA marketed for sale with multiple use scenarios
Property enables condo conversion, medical-adjacent use, or long-term land banking; demisable into six pods
Extensive capital improvements (HVAC, roof, electrical, finishes) reduce execution risk for buyer seeking alternative uses
stories.summary: Stream Realty Partners is marketing 20532 El Toro, a recently renovated three-story, 61,418-SF office building in Mission Viejo, California. The property is currently configured as a single-tenant space but offers flexibility to be demised into six pods for multi-tenant use, or repurposed for medical, condo conversion, or land banking. Recent capital investments in systems and finishes are intended to attract institutional or opportunistic buyers.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily relevance. The condo conversion and land-banking optionality suggest the South Orange County market may see selective office-to-residential conversion, but the property's scale and location (interior office park in Mission Viejo, not an urban infill site) make it a lower-priority target for apartment development compared to urban core opportunities.

stories.title: Mott Haven Development Site Set for One or More Residential Buildings
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets arranged the $21.7-million sale of 122 Bruckner Blvd., a 172,774-square-foot residential development site in the Mott Haven section of the Bronx. JLL represented the seller, Altmark Group, and negotiated the transaction directly with the buyer, Nalcorp, which plans to develop one or more residential buildings. The JLL Capital Markets Investment Sales and Advisory team that arranged the transaction was led by Brendan Maddigan, Ethan Stanton and Mike Mazzara. “The combination of substantial development rights, strong connectivity to Manhattan and a compelling multifamily market story generated significant interest from investors,” said Maddigan. “The neighborhood has experienced tremendous residential growth over the past decade, and the buyer is well positioned to capitalize on increased renter demand.” The property sits just south of the Major Deegan Expressway and two blocks east of the Willis Avenue Bridge, providing connectivity to Manhattan and the broader New York metropolitan area. The post Mott Haven Development Site Set for One or More Residential Buildings appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Nalcorp acquires 172,774-SF Mott Haven development site for $21.7M; plans one or more residential buildings
Location: 122 Bruckner Blvd., Bronx, near Major Deegan Expressway and Willis Avenue Bridge; strong Manhattan connectivity
Neighborhood experienced significant residential growth over past decade; strong renter demand attracts multifamily investors
stories.summary: JLL Capital Markets arranged the $21.7-million sale of a 172,774-SF residential development site at 122 Bruckner Boulevard in the Mott Haven section of the Bronx. Buyer Nalcorp plans to develop one or more residential buildings on the site, which benefits from proximity to the Major Deegan Expressway and Willis Avenue Bridge for Manhattan connectivity. The transaction was driven by the neighborhood's strong residential growth trajectory and sustained multifamily demand.
stories.impact: Directly supports supply-side multifamily development in outer boroughs. Mott Haven's decade-long residential growth and sustained renter demand justify continued ground acquisition and development; Bronx gentrification and Manhattan overflow demand remain tailwinds for apartment supply in this submarket. Monitor Nalcorp's timeline and unit count—early indicator of confidence in Bronx multifamily economics at current underwriting assumptions.

stories.title: Former IHOP Site in Pacific Beach Trades as Redevelopment Opportunity
stories.excerpt: Whitewater Commercial Real Estate, a San Diego-based commercial real estate brokerage specializing in multifamily investment sales and development opportunities, closed the sale of 856 Grand Ave., a redevelopment site located in Pacific Beach. The property sold for $8,000,000. Whitewater epresented the buyer, 856 Grand Ave LLC. David Kern of The Mansour Group represented the seller, IMS PB LLC. “Development opportunities of this scale are exceptionally rare in Pacific Beach, particularly along Grand Avenue and within walking distance of the beach,” said Whitewater president Carson Trujillo. “The acquisition provides a unique opportunity to reposition a highly visible coastal property in one of San Diego’s most supply-constrained neighborhoods.” Formerly occupied by IHOP, the site is situated approximately one block from the Pacific Ocean. The buyer plans to explore multifamily redevelopment opportunities for the property, taking advantage of its premier location within one of Southern California’s most desirable coastal communities. The post Former IHOP Site in Pacific Beach Trades as Redevelopment Opportunity appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: 856 Grand Ave., Pacific Beach, CA trades for $8M; former IHOP redevelopment site one block from ocean
Buyer plans multifamily redevelopment; site described as exceptionally rare in supply-constrained Pacific Beach neighborhood
Premier coastal location and scarcity of development sites drive acquisition despite high per-SF land cost
stories.summary: A former IHOP site at 856 Grand Avenue in Pacific Beach, San Diego sold for $8 million to a multifamily developer (856 Grand Ave LLC). The property is located approximately one block from the Pacific Ocean in one of Southern California's most supply-constrained coastal neighborhoods. The buyer intends to explore multifamily redevelopment, capitalizing on the property's visibility and scarcity of comparable opportunities.
stories.impact: Strong demand signal for coastal San Diego multifamily supply. Pacific Beach's supply constraints and desirability create favorable conditions for adaptive reuse and ground-up development; the $8M acquisition price for a single-building footprint reflects land scarcity and rental demand in this micromarket. Expect elevated hard costs and extended entitlement timelines—typical for coastal SoCal projects—but strong absorption assumptions if executed as mid-market rentals.

stories.title: Naftali Credit Partners Arranges $75M Debt for Flatbush Condo Project
stories.excerpt: Naftali Credit Partners has closed on a $75-million loan to support the development of 757 Flatbush Ave., a nine-story ground-up condominium development in Brooklyn’s Flatbush neighborhood. The financing package includes a $58-million senior loan provided by Madison Realty Capital and a $17-million mezzanine loan provided by Naftali Credit Partners. Following a successful collaboration on 208 Delancey in 2020 and 24-01 Queens Plaza North in 2024, New Empire, a New York-based real estate development firm led by Bentey Zhou, has re-engaged with Naftali Credit Partners to finance 757 Flatbush Ave. The loan refinances existing land debt and funds the remaining hard and soft costs. “Brooklyn continues to be an important focus for residential development, particularly as demand remains strong for high-quality condominium projects,” said Glenn Grimaldi, CEO of Naftali Credit Partners. “At 757 Flatbush Avenue, New Empire is advancing a thoughtfully designed development that we believe is well aligned with the market.” The post Naftali Credit Partners Arranges $75M Debt for Flatbush Condo Project appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Naftali Credit Partners closes $75M loan package for 757 Flatbush Ave. nine-story ground-up condo development
Financing: $58M senior (Madison Realty Capital) + $17M mezzanine (Naftali); refinances land debt and funds remaining costs
Developer New Empire has repeat relationship with Naftali; strong Brooklyn condo demand cited by lender as confidence signal
stories.summary: Naftali Credit Partners closed a $75-million financing package ($58M senior + $17M mezzanine) for 757 Flatbush Avenue, a nine-story ground-up condominium development in Brooklyn's Flatbush neighborhood. The loan refinances existing land debt and funds hard and soft costs for the project, which is being developed by New York-based New Empire (led by Bentley Zhou). The lender attributed the investment to strong Brooklyn condo market fundamentals and repeat developer confidence.
stories.impact: Signals continued multifamily capital availability and condo-market strength in Brooklyn despite broader CRE headwinds. The $75M financing on a nine-story project implies strong lender underwriting assumptions for Brooklyn rental/condo absorption and exit rents. Brooklyn's condo market remains a preferred target for both debt and equity capital; this approval may encourage competing sponsors to accelerate Flatbush and adjacent submarkets (Prospect Heights, Crown Heights) development pipelines.

stories.title: Content Creation Platform Spotter Takes 17K SF at Thor’s 25 West 39th Street
stories.excerpt: Upload complete. Spotter, a platform that provides tools to help content creators post on YouTube, has signed a 17,000-square-foot lease at Thor Equities’ 25 West 39th Street in Midtown. The lease spans the entire 14th floor of the 16-story office building one block south of Bryant Park, according to Crain’s New York Business, which first reported the deal. The deal is a three-year lease with an asking rent of approximately $70 per square foot. It’s unclear whether the deal represents a relocation or an inaugural New York office for Spotter, which has its headquarters in Los Angeles, according to its website. Landlord Thor Equities was represented in the deal by Christopher Okada and Thomas Capuano from Okada & Company. Okada did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Josh Berg from Newmark, who represented the tenant on this lease, declined to comment. A spokesperson for Spotter did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Built in 1907, 25 West 39th Street served as the Engineering Society’s building until it was converted into an office in 1961. Thor Equities acquired the midblock building in 2006 for $82 million, according to property records. It’s unclear when Spotter will move into its new Midtown offices, but it will join several other office tenants at 25 west 39th Street, including retailer Movado Group and art collective Tin Drum. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Spotter (content-creation platform) leases 17,000 SF on entire 14th floor of 25 West 39th St., Midtown Manhattan
Three-year lease at ~$70/SF; one block south of Bryant Park; unclear if relocation or inaugural NYC office
Tech/media tenant demand for Midtown office space persists despite broader sector weakness; tenant choice signals quality/location value
stories.summary: Spotter, a YouTube content-creator platform, signed a 17,000-SF lease for the entire 14th floor at Thor Equities' 25 West 39th Street in Midtown Manhattan at approximately $70/SF on a three-year term. The property is located one block south of Bryant Park. It is unclear whether the lease represents a relocation for Spotter (HQ'd in Los Angeles) or an inaugural New York office.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily impact. Office leasing in Midtown does not directly drive apartment demand, but sustained demand from tech/media tenants for premium Manhattan office space suggests continued professional-class employment concentration in Manhattan, which historically supports residential demand in adjacent neighborhoods (Noho, East Village, Long Island City). Monitor whether Spotter's NYC presence signals broader content-industry clustering—a second-order driver of young professional housing demand.

stories.title: Changes in Retail Real Estate Nationwide Have Accelerated: Forum
stories.excerpt: Kicking off a panel on the nature of experiential retail and the future of destination developments during Commercial Observer’s recent Retail & Hospitality Forum, Nima Fazeli, senior vice president of development for the H.wood Group, made a statement that should have seemed more shocking than it was. “I don’t think you can really differentiate the different asset classes anymore,” Fazeli said of commercial real estate in general. “I think everybody in this room would agree that it’s all becoming blended into one homogeneous experience.” This simple statement, a dismantling of sorts of CRE’s conventional sector and asset class divisions, was just one at the event — which took place on June 18 at the Paramount Club at 1301 Avenue of the Americas in Midtown — indicating the sweeping changes engulfing the retail sector in 2026. After introductory remarks from Peter Brindley, head of real estate for building owner Elecor Properties (formerly Paramount Group), and the day’s emcee, Brian Pascus, a finance reporter for Commercial Observer, the event’s first panel included a discussion of curating leading retail corridors and the resurgence of Manhattan’s Upper East Side. David Koeppel, a partner at law firm Koeppel Rosen, noted that since COVID the area has developed a new sense of cool. “Post-COVID, there was somewhat of a void on the Upper East Side. People had left,” said Koeppel. “There were great values in residential real estate to be had, and those voids were filled by younger people starting families. Retailers like that. They love the foot traffic created by the residential tenants.” Rachel Abeles, senior vice president for customer and revenue growth for Bloomingdale’s, said the Upper East Side fixture has spent the last few years seeking to bring more ambition into their platform with an emphasis on digital growth. David Koeppel (from left), Rachel Abeles, Karen Bellantoni, and Jackie Totolo sit on a panel at the Commercial Observer Retail and H
bullets: Panel discussion at CRE Forum highlights blending of retail, office, hospitality asset classes into experiential mixed-use
Industry consensus that traditional CRE sector divisions are dissolving; retail reinvention toward experiential/destination use accelerating
Implications: single-use retail declining; adaptive reuse and mixed-use development increasingly favor bundled commercial + residential projects
stories.summary: A Commercial Observer Retail & Hospitality Forum panel (June 18, 2026) featured industry experts discussing the collapse of traditional asset-class distinctions in commercial real estate. H.wood Group SVP Nima Fazeli stated that the industry can no longer differentiate between retail, office, hospitality, and other sectors—all are converging into blended experiential environments. The panel indicated sweeping structural changes in the retail sector in 2026.
stories.impact: Significant strategic implication for multifamily developers with mixed-use aspirations. The erosion of single-use retail and shift toward experiential/destination properties increases demand for ground-floor programming and bundled retail-residential developments, particularly in urban infill submarkets. Developers should expect ground-floor retail/amenity rents to remain compressed; compensate with higher residential unit rents and premium amenity offerings. This trend also signals ongoing weakness in traditional neighborhood retail and higher risk for lease-up and stabilization of purely retail components in mixed-use projects.

stories.title: Bread of Life starts another YIGBY project in Houston
stories.excerpt: One of Houston’s best-known YIGBY developers teased plans for another project. Bread of Life Incorporated, a charitable organization associated with Beyoncé’s former church in Houston, plans to start work next month on The Weirton, a seven-story, 99-unit supportive housing facility to be constructed at 3208 Austin Street, according to a project filing submitted to the state. The 82,000-square-foot building is scheduled for completion in September 2027, and the estimated cost of construction is $20 million, or about $202,000 per unit. Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation Filings are preliminary and subject to change. The Midtown development will include 3,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Bread of Life (church-affiliated developer) starts 99-unit supportive housing project in Midtown Houston; $20M construction cost (~$202k/unit)
Seven-story building at 3208 Austin St.; 82k SF; completion Sept. 2027; includes 3k SF ground-floor commercial + 65 parking spaces
Fifth project in Houston area for Bread of Life; YIGBY model (supportive/low-income housing) with ancillary commercial and generous parking
stories.summary: Bread of Life Incorporated (a charity affiliated with Beyoncé's former church in Houston) is launching construction on The Weirton, a seven-story, 99-unit supportive housing facility in Midtown Houston at 3208 Austin Street. The $20-million project (~$202k/unit) is scheduled for completion in September 2027 and includes 3,000 SF of ground-floor commercial space, dining areas, and 65 parking spaces. This is Bread of Life's fifth Houston-area project and follows the success of Knowles Temenos Place Apartments (Hurricane Katrina relief housing).
stories.impact: Limited impact on market-rate multifamily development economics. Bread of Life's supportive housing model operates under different financing (likely grant/philanthropic + government subsidy) than market-rate development and thus does not directly compete on underwriting assumptions. However, the project signals continued investor/philanthropic capital appetite for below-market housing in Houston and demonstrates that Midtown Houston infrastructure can support multi-story, mixed-use residential projects—a positive signal for adjacent market-rate or workforce development.

stories.title: Kosher Grocer Bingo Wholesale Buys $50M Dev Site in Bed-Stuy
stories.excerpt: Popular kosher grocery chain Bingo Wholesale is expanding its Brooklyn footprint into Bedford-Stuyvesant. Bingo’s holding company, Norworth Holdings, carried out a $50 million acquisition across three industrial property transactions along Warsoff Place and Walworth Street, according to property records filed Thursday. The selling entities, LFFT TIC and EFFT TIC, are tied to members of the Follman family, who owned the parcels. Abraham Follman served as the entity’s signatory. Among the transactions was the purchase of a $25 million commercial condo at 18 Warsoff Place, at the corner of Flushing Avenue. The industrial building spans 37,916 square feet and neighbors Bingo’s second $14 million acquisition at 39 Walworth Street. The 69,747-square-foot industrial building straddles both blocks between Flushing and Park avenues. An unaddressed Walworth Street property next door — the tax lot of which measures 13,613 square feet — was also included in the deal for $11 million. Additional air rights across the transactions totaled 172,796 square feet, according to PincusCo. The transaction is expected to result in Bingo’s fifth supermarket location. Its kosher offerings will serve Bed-Stuy’s growing Jewish community. Bingo’s stores in Brooklyn, Manhattan’s Inwood neighborhood, Monsey, N.Y., and Lakewood, N.J., all serve large Orthodox Jewish populations. Official site plans for the new Bed-Stuy spot, however, have not yet been disclosed by the grocer. Representatives for Bingo did not respond to a request for comment. The multi-parcel transaction has been years in the making, PincusCo reported, as now-resolved litigation within the Follman family sought to block the deal. Follman could not immediately be reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bingo Wholesale (kosher grocer) acquires $50M industrial portfolio in Bed-Stuy, Brooklyn across three properties
Transactions: $25M condo at 18 Warsoff Pl. (37,916 SF) + $14M at 39 Walworth St. (69,747 SF) + $11M adjacent lot (13,613 SF)
Seller entities tied to Follman family; consolidation of industrial footprint along Warsoff/Walworth suggests retail/logistics vertical integration or future development optionality
stories.summary: Bingo Wholesale's holding company, Norworth Holdings, acquired a $50-million industrial portfolio across three contiguous parcels in Bed-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn along Warsoff Place and Walworth Street. The acquisitions include a $25M commercial condo at 18 Warsoff Place (37,916 SF), a $14M building at 39 Walworth Street (69,747 SF), and an $11M air-rights parcel (13,613 SF). The seller was the Follman family. The consolidation suggests either operational expansion or future land monetization.
stories.impact: Potential signal of emerging ground-up residential or mixed-use development opportunity in Bed-Stuyvesant. Bingo Wholesale's $50M consolidation of contiguous industrial parcels could indicate: (1) supply-chain/logistics vertical integration for grocery operations, or (2) assembly for future development disposition (sale-leaseback, ground lease, or joint venture with residential developer). If Bingo is assembling for disposition, monitor for residential development announcements in Bed-Stuy—a rapidly gentrifying Brooklyn submarket. The scale ($50M) and contiguity suggest institutional-grade redevelopment potential.

stories.title: Nashville Zoo Proving a Beast of an Opponent for Data Center
stories.excerpt: Data centers sometimes want the lion’s share of available resources. In what might be a first for a U.S. teeming with data center opposition, it’s a zoo that’s fighting back. On May 20, it was reported that DC Blox, an Atlanta-based digital infrastructure provider, intends to build a 69,000-square-foot data center at 648 Grassmere Park in Nashville’s Radnor neighborhood. If approved, the development would be built just yards from the Nashville Zoo — something the animal care facility is actively fighting against. The zoo, at 3777 Nolensville Pike, is home to over 3,700 animals, all of which the facility claims would be negatively impacted by the construction and utilization of the data center. Earlier this month, the zoo launched a petition against the building of the data center. Over Father’s Day weekend, the petition reached more than half a million signatures. “If the data center is built, it would have detrimental effects on our animals and the people who call South Nashville home,” Leah Trice, a representative for the Nashville Zoo, told Commercial Observer. “The property line of the proposed data center is only 320 feet from the breeding facilities of our top conservation species, the clouded leopard. These elusive animals are extremely sensitive to mechanical noises.” Trice added that the prolonged noise pollution caused by the proposed data center’s construction would cause health problems for the zoo’s animals, as well as introduce pollutants to the animal and human environment. “Stormwater runoff from this new construction would cause worse damage to our already fragile retention pond,” she said of the area, which already struggles with flooding. “The property’s water runoff goes directly into our watershed, potentially washing in pollutants that could harm our animals and the people who visit the zoo every day.” The zoo’s lawyers are in contact with DC Blox, Trice said. The zoo has until July 6 to stop the data center from being built. DC Blox did not im
bullets: DC Blox (data center operator) proposes 69k SF data center at 648 Grassmere Park, Nashville, near Nashville Zoo
Nashville Zoo (3,777 animals) launched petition against project; petition exceeded 500k signatures; zoo claims project would harm animals and South Nashville community
Opposition highlights NIMBYism risk for industrial/logistics development near residential/recreational amenities; rare case of non-residential entity opposing data center
stories.summary: Atlanta-based data center operator DC Blox proposed a 69,000-SF data center at 648 Grassmere Park in Nashville's Radnor neighborhood, located yards from the Nashville Zoo at 3777 Nolensville Pike. The zoo launched a petition against the project, which garnered over 500,000 signatures by mid-June. Zoo leadership claims the data center would negatively impact over 3,700 animals and the broader South Nashville community.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact but important signal for industrial/logistics site selection in high-visibility submarkets. Data center opposition is typically residential-NIMBY driven; a zoo's successful mobilization of 500k+ signatures represents a new entitlement-risk vector for non-residential industrial projects nationwide. Developers targeting industrial/logistics sites in growing metros should conduct early environmental and community-use-adjacency due diligence. Indirect implication: if Grassmere Park site is blocked, demand for alternative data center sites in Nashville may increase land prices for remaining industrial zoned parcels, affecting construction costs for any adjacent multifamily projects.

stories.title: Moinian Group Signs Deals With Three AI Firms at 60 Madison Avenue
stories.excerpt: The Moinian Group has been dealing with an avalanche of new leasing in Midtown over the last two weeks. The office landlord said it has signed 68,000 square feet of new leases with four new tenants — three technology firms and a communications company — at 60 Madison Avenue, with web developer Tenex Labs taking the largest slice at 25,000 square feet. The Moinian Group did not disclose the asking rent for the building or the length of the Tenex lease, but office asking rents in Midtown South averaged $86.38 per square foot in May, according to an office market report from CBRE. Tenex’s new office will span the entire 12th floor and penthouse level of the building, but it is unclear where the firm is relocating from. “60 Madison Avenue, with its prime Midtown South location directly on Madison Square Park, continues to attract innovative and fast-growing companies seeking high-quality office space in one of Manhattan’s most dynamic business districts,” Joseph Moinian, founder and CEO at the Moinian Group, said in a statement. “These leases, all signed within the last two weeks, demonstrate the strong demand for thoughtfully designed, move-in-ready spaces and build upon the momentum we have established at the property throughout 2026.” CBRE’s Gregg Rothkin, Hayden Pascal, Jared London, Taylor Walker and Keegan Schenk represented Moinian Group in all four deals, while Noel Flagg and E.N. Cutler of Newmark negotiated on behalf of Tenex. In a separate deal, Pace, a firm that develops artificial intelligence agentic workforce for insurance companies with backing from Sequoia Capital and Thrive Capital, also signed a lease for 17,500 square feet across the entire seventh floor of 60 Madison Avenue, according to the landlord. The deal marks a relocation and expansion from 734 Broadway, where Pace occupies 3,500 square feet, according to Nicholas Markel of Cresa, who represented the tenant “We’re customizing a prebuilt, they need space to accommodate their growing team
bullets: Moinian Group signs 68k SF leases (four tenants) at 60 Madison Ave., Midtown South; three tech firms + one comms company
Largest lease: Tenex Labs, 25k SF (entire 12th floor + penthouse); no rent or term disclosed; market asking rent ~$86.38/SF (May 2026)
Back-to-back leasing activity in Midtown South signals recovery momentum for premium office in high-amenity submarkets despite sector headwinds
stories.summary: The Moinian Group reported signing four leases totaling 68,000 SF at 60 Madison Avenue in Midtown South Manhattan over two weeks, comprising three technology firms and a communications company. Web developer Tenex Labs took the largest lease at 25,000 SF (entire 12th floor and penthouse); specific rent and term were not disclosed. Midtown South office asking rents averaged $86.38/SF in May 2026. The building's location directly on Madison Square Park was cited as a key draw for growth-oriented companies.
stories.impact: Positive signal for Midtown South office stabilization but limited direct multifamily relevance. However, the convergence of tech tenant leasing in premium Midtown South locations (near Madison Square Park, Union Square) historically correlates with residential demand from tech-industry professionals in adjacent neighborhoods (Flatiron, Gramercy, Union Square, East Village, LIC). Strong office leasing velocity in Midtown South may accelerate demand for luxury rental housing within 10–15 minute commutes; monitor for secondary residential spillover to outer-borough tech hubs (Williamsburg, Long Island City).

stories.title: Domus lands approval for standard-breaking Fulton Market apartments
stories.excerpt: Chicago City Council greenlit a Fulton Market development that could set a new density precedent for the area.  Previously, zoning officials sought to reject a plan for the planned high-rise to gain a DX-16 zoning designation, which is normally reserved for skyscrapers. The new development at 215 North Racine Avenue is set to total 29 stories and 347 apartment units. A nearby parcel of unused land slated to be transformed into a public park was also purchased by the Domus Real Estate Group, the project’s developer, to fulfill a condition of the city’s approval. A small delay in acquiring the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Domus Real Estate Group wins Chicago City Council approval for 347-unit, 29-story apartment tower at 215 North Racine, Fulton Market
Project granted DX-16 (skyscraper) zoning—highest density designation; typically reserved for downtown supertalls; previously rejected by zoning officials

Developer acquired adjacent vacant land for public park to satisfy city conditions; approval sets potential precedent for higher density in Fulton Market, raising neighborhood-integration and infrastructure concerns
stories.summary: Chicago City Council approved Domus Real Estate Group's 347-unit, 29-story apartment development at 215 North Racine in Fulton Market after overcoming initial zoning official rejection. The project received a DX-16 zoning designation (typically reserved for skyscrapers), which was not standard for the area. Domus purchased an adjacent parcel for public park development to satisfy city approval conditions. Critics argue the decision sets a density precedent that could reshape Fulton Market's coordinated development strategy.
stories.impact: Significant supply-side signal for Chicago multifamily development in hot submarkets. Fulton Market's proven resilience post-pandemic and strong fundamentals created political will to override traditional density guidelines. This approval signals: (1) Chicago is willing to greenlight higher-density residential in strong-demand neighborhoods to address housing shortage; (2) Fulton Market's market fundamentals justify supertall apartment development; (3) competing developers may now seek similar DX-16 designations for other Fulton Market or adjacent submarket sites (West Loop, Pilsen). Monitor for copycat applications and potential infrastructure strain (parking, transit, schools) that could trigger community backlash and extend entitlement timelines for follow-on projects.

stories.title: “Straight out of Las Vegas”: Developers complete E11even Residences in Miami
stories.excerpt: E11even Partners and PMG completed their first E11even-branded condo tower, and have recorded nearly all of the unit closings, according to The Real Deal’s analysis of property records.  The 65-story tower at 20 Northeast 11th Street in Miami, across the street from the E11even nightclub in downtown’s Park West district, was the first high-rise condo tower in Miami to sell out in the post-pandemic frenzy. The developers reserved 90 percent of units at E11even Club Hotel & Residences Miami within two months of presales in 2021. The building has 457 units.  E11even Partners is led by Marc Roberts, Michael Simkins […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: E11even Partners and PMG complete 457-unit, 65-story luxury condo tower in Miami Downtown (Park West); ~$375M total sellout
Building sold out post-presales (90% reserved within 2 months of 2021 launch); ~400 closings recorded; second tower planned with sky-bridge connection
Amenity package includes E11even nightclub/lounge, 22-hot-tub pool deck, casino-style sports lounge, rooftop bar, luxury spa, branded food concepts; hotel component opens later in 2026
stories.summary: E11even Partners and PMG completed E11even Club Hotel & Residences Miami, a 65-story, 457-unit luxury condo tower at 20 Northeast 11th Street in Downtown Miami's Park West district, directly across from the E11even nightclub. The building achieved rapid sellout (90% reserved within two months of 2021 presales) and recorded approximately 400 deed closings. Total sellout is approximately $375 million. The building features casino-resort-style amenities (after-hours lounge, day club, 22-hot-tub pool, sports lounge, rooftop bar, luxury spa) and will connect to a second tower via sky bridge. An integrated hotel component opens later in 2026.
stories.impact: Strong demand indicator for ultra-luxury condo/residential development in Miami's downtown core. E11even's rapid presale absorption and $375M gross revenue demonstrate continued appetite for branded, experiential luxury housing units in Miami. The integration of nightlife, hotel, and dining concepts reflects broader industry trend (Article 7) toward blended-use residential properties. This project may establish a pricing/amenity ceiling for Miami's downtown condo market; competing developers should expect elevated buyer expectations for signature amenities and branded experiences. Strong absorption also validates continued investor/foreign-buyer demand for Miami trophy assets, supporting development finance availability in the submarket.

stories.title: The challenge of cheap markets
stories.excerpt: Studios for $1,050 a month, one-bedrooms for $1,310 and two-bedrooms for $1,525. Those are actual asking rents for “high end,” downtown, market-rate units in the Erdace Apartments, “the epitome of a luxe residence.” As you might guess, they are not in New York City. They’re in downtown Lake Charles, Louisiana. These markets might as well be on different planets. A developer in Gotham would make a killing if he could build luxury rentals for $160,000 per unit, which in 2018 is what the Erdace Apartments were projected to cost. A developer building at that price in Lake Charles, by contrast, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Erdace Apartments (Lake Charles, LA): luxury 270-unit complex built 2020 at ~$160k/unit; rents capped ($1,050 studios to $1,525 2-BR)
Muss Development (NYC) and Amesbury Companies (Baton Rouge) acquire property via HUD-insured loan at $137k/unit; plan light renovation and rebrand as Ryan Apartments

Economics illustration: identical-quality luxury apartment achieves $2.7M gross revenue in Lake Charles vs. multiples higher in NYC; cost-rent disconnect in secondary markets creates acquisition opportunity for institutional capital
stories.summary: The 270-unit Erdace Apartments in downtown Lake Charles, Louisiana, built in 2020 for approximately $160,000 per unit with premium finishes (dishwashers, 10-foot ceilings, granite, stainless steel, balconies) and amenities (clubhouse, lounge, fitness center, pool), commands asking rents of $1,050 (studios), $1,310 (1-BR), and $1,525 (2-BR). In June 2024, New York-based Muss Development and Baton Rouge-based Amesbury Companies acquired the property via HUD-insured loan at $137,000 per unit—a $23k/unit discount. The partners plan light capital improvements (lobby, facade, hallways, balconies) and rebrand as Ryan Apartments.
stories.impact: Illustrates structural arbitrage opportunity in secondary-market multifamily acquisitions for institutional capital. Lake Charles' $137k/unit acquisition price and $1.5k/month 2-BR rents (4.1% yield pre-operating costs) are not viable for new development but highly attractive for value-add repositioning by experienced operators (Muss is NYC-based with 60+ East Coast assets). This strategy signals a bifurcation in multifamily capital deployment: primary markets (NYC, LA, Miami) support new development, while secondary markets face capital constraints, lower rent growth, and compressed valuations. Developers in tight-supply primary markets should monitor whether institutional capital increasingly targets secondary-market acquisitions, potentially reducing debt/equity availability for new ground-up development in Tier 1 cities.

stories.title: Gauging the Obama Center’s real estate ripple effects across the South Side
stories.excerpt: The opening of the Obama Presidential Center on the Juneteenth holiday marks the culmination of a project that had construction costs balloon from an initial $300 million estimate to a staggering $850 million, following a half-decade legal battle with park preservation advocates, and pandemic-related construction delays. Envisioned as a 19-acre economic engine for Chicago’s South Side, the center features a towering 225-foot museum building wrapped in concrete letters from the former two-term president’s historic Selma speech. While the center’s backers have promised it would be a long-term boon for tourism and the area’s economic infrastructure, it has simultaneously accelerated real […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Obama Presidential Center (Chicago South Side) opens with $850M construction cost (vs. $300M estimate); 19-acre economic development engine
East Woodlawn home prices doubled from 2019–2025 to $440k median; outside developers acquiring lots for near-$1M townhome development
Speculation-driven displacement accelerated housing costs; construction delays and legal challenges (park preservation) extended project timeline; legislative battles over tenant rights ongoing
stories.summary: The Obama Presidential Center opened on Chicago's South Side (Juneteenth 2024) with construction costs of $850 million—nearly triple the initial $300-million estimate—following a five-year legal battle with park preservation advocates and pandemic-related delays. Envisioned as a 19-acre economic engine, the 225-foot museum building features concrete letters from Obama's Selma speech. Real estate spillover effects include: East Woodlawn home prices doubled from 2019–2025 (median $440k), out-of-state speculation accelerated, and developers acquired vacant lots to build boxy near-$1M townhomes. These changes have sparked intense legislative battles over tenant rights and displacement.
stories.impact: Cautionary signal on speculative real estate dynamics in historic neighborhoods adjacent to anchor institutions. The Obama Center's opening catalyzed rapid gentrification and asset price appreciation in East Woodlawn and adjacent South Side neighborhoods, attracting outside speculative capital and driving displacement. For multifamily developers: (1) institutional anchors (museums, universities, corporate HQs) can accelerate neighborhood transition but often trigger unplanned speculation and zoning tension; (2) development costs (site assembly, construction inflation, legal/entitlement delays) can balloon 2-3x, eroding underwriting assumptions; (3) local backlash against perceived displacement may trigger regulatory constraints (tenant protections, inclusionary zoning) that compress development margins. South Side neighborhoods now see heightened regulatory risk; developers should model stricter inclusionary zoning and extended entitlement timelines in anchor-adjacent submarkets.
